name: BRICS
headquarters: Shanghai, China (New Development Bank); no permanent secretariat

governance.decision_making: Consensus among members; no binding treaty obligations

strategic_priorities: De-dollarization: expanding local currency trade settlement and BRICS Pay platform
New Development Bank: scaling infrastructure lending as alternative to IMF/World Bank
Reform of global governance institutions — IMF, UN Security Council, WTO
Energy cooperation: coordinating among major fossil fuel producers and importers
Technology and AI cooperation outside Western export control frameworks
Managing a heterogeneous membership with deeply divergent interests and systems
summary: BRICS expanded in January 2024 to ten members, adding Iran, Egypt, Ethiopia, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE, transforming the bloc from a loose emerging-market forum into a broad coalition spanning major oil exporters, the Global South, and revisionist powers. China and Russia champion the bloc as a vehicle for multipolarity and dollar alternatives, while India, Brazil, and Gulf states pursue a more pragmatic, non-aligned posture. Internal cohesion is limited, but the grouping's economic weight gives it agenda-setting influence.
recent_developments: BRICS Pay cross-border payment pilot expanded to 7 member countries in 2025, processing over $12 billion in transactions; dollar share of intra-BRICS trade fell to 34%
Brazil's 2025 BRICS chairmanship prioritized climate finance reform and Global South debt restructuring at the UN Summit of the Future
New Development Bank approved $14 billion in project financing in 2025, with growing portfolio in renewable energy across Africa and South Asia
Saudi Arabia and UAE participation remained cautious: both declined to endorse anti-Western statements while advancing bilateral ties with China
Indonesia, Malaysia, and Vietnam submitted formal interest in BRICS partnership status; expansion criteria under negotiation for 2026
risks: India-China strategic rivalry limits substantive military or intelligence cooperation
Iran's membership complicates relations with Gulf state members and risks secondary sanctions exposure
No binding dispute-resolution mechanism; geopolitical rifts (e.g., Ethiopia-Egypt over Nile) remain unresolved within the group
De-dollarization progress is slow; US dollar still dominates global commodity pricing and reserves
